31: A direct revelation: 'Behold the days are coming when I will make a new covenant with the house of Israel and the house of Judah.' The new covenant is the Christological center of the OT's prophetic program: Jesus at the Last Supper explicitly claims to enact this covenant (Luke 22:20: 'This cup that is poured out for you is the new covenant in my blood'), and Hebrews quotes all of Jeremiah 31:31-34 (Jeremiah 8:8-12) as the scriptural proof that the old covenant's priesthood and sacrificial system were provisional and superseded. The three elements of the new covenant are fulfilled in Christ: (1) law on hearts → the Spirit writes Christ's character in the believer; (2) universal knowledge of YHWH → all who come to Christ know the Father (John 17:3); (3) permanent forgiveness → the once-for-all sacrifice of Christ (Hebrews 9:26-28; Jeremiah 10:14).
1: The opening oracle — "at that time" I will be the God of all the clans of Israel and they will be my people — uses the eschatological time-marker baʿet hahiʿ to signal that what follows transcends the immediate historical moment. The covenant formula "I will be their God and they will be my people" runs from the Sinai narrative (Exodus 6:7) through Jeremiah to its NT fulfillment: Revelation 21:3 declares, "Behold, the dwelling of God is with man. He will dwell with them and they will be his people, and God himself will be with them as their God." Christ's incarnation is the hinge on which the covenant formula passes from promise to fulfillment.
2: The recollection of finding grace in the wilderness — Israel going to its resting place, YHWH appearing from afar — grounds the new-covenant hope in the original Exodus pattern. The wilderness-grace motif recurs in the NT: John the Baptist (Matthew 3:1-3) appears in the wilderness as the voice preparing the way; Jesus himself is led into the wilderness (Matthew 4:1) and emerges to begin his redemptive mission. Every new act of divine grace follows the Exodus pattern: wilderness first, then the rest of the promised land.
3: I have loved you with an everlasting love; therefore I have drawn you with lovingkindness — the declaration of ʾahavat ʿolam (everlasting love) and ḥesed (covenant loyalty). Paul's argument in Romans 8:38-39 is the NT counterpart: nothing can separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus. The "everlasting love" that Jeremiah announces as the foundation of the new-covenant hope is precisely the love that Christ embodies and enacts in his incarnation and death (John 13:1: "having loved his own... he loved them to the end").
4: The promise to build you again, O virgin Israel, so that she takes up her tambourines and dances — the image of the restored community as rebuilt and rejoicing. Revelation 19:7-9 presents the eschatological counterpart: the wedding of the Lamb where the bride has made herself ready. The virgin Israel who dances with tambourines is a type of the church presented as a pure bride to Christ (2 Corinthians 11:2; Ephesians 5:27). The rebuilding of the community anticipates the building of the church Christ promises (Matthew 16:18).
5: The planting of vineyards on the mountains of Samaria and eating their fruit — the restoration of the northern kingdom — points to the eschatological gathering that includes both Israel and Judah. Jesus's parable of the vineyard (Matthew 20:1-16; Jeremiah 21:28-41) uses vine-planting as the image of God's kingdom project. The vine imagery culminates in John 15:1: "I am the true vine" — Christ himself is the vineyard that Israel's planting anticipated, and through him all nations share in the fruit.
6: The promise that watchmen in Ephraim will call out: Rise and let us go up to Zion to YHWH our God — the northern tribes returning to worship in Jerusalem — anticipates the gathering of all Israel under one shepherd. Jesus presents himself as the good shepherd who gathers the scattered sheep (John 10:16: "I have other sheep not of this fold; I must bring them also"). The call from Ephraim to go up to Zion is fulfilled when the Gentiles and dispersed Israel are gathered into the one flock under Christ, the one shepherd.
7: The command to shout for Jacob with joy, rejoice over the chief of the nations — the nations called to celebrate Israel's restoration — is an eschatological jubilee. The NT presents the ultimate jubilee as Christ's redemption: Luke 4:18-19, where Jesus reads from Isaiah 61 and announces the year of the Lord's favor, declares the jubilee Christ inaugurates. The nations shouting for joy over Jacob finds its fulfillment in the nations rejoicing in the gospel (Romans 15:10-12, citing Deuteronomy 32:43 and Psalms 117:1).
8: YHWH gathering his people from the north country and from the ends of the earth — including the blind, the lame, the pregnant, and the nursing mother — is the great eschatological ingathering. Jesus's healing of the blind (Matthew 9:27-31; Mark 10:46-52; John 9) and the lame (Mark 2:1-12; John 5:1-15) are the inaugurated signs of this gathering. Isaiah 35:5-6 (the blind see, the lame leap) and Luke 7:22 (Jesus's answer to John the Baptist) establish that the messianic era is marked by precisely these reversals. Christ's healing ministry is the advance sign of this worldwide gathering.
9: The promise that they shall come with weeping and with pleas for mercy; I will lead them by a straight way, for I am a father to Israel, and Ephraim is my firstborn — the divine fatherhood and the return of the lost son. Jesus's parable of the prodigal son (Luke 15:11-32) draws directly on this imagery: the returning son comes in weeping penitence; the father runs to him and restores him. The explicit affirmation "I am a father to Israel" is the theological ground for Jesus's teaching about the Father's love for returning sinners. The "straight way" (derek yashar) is the path of restoration that Jesus himself claims to be (John 14:6).
10: The nations called to hear — He who scattered Israel will gather him and will keep him as a shepherd keeps his flock — is the divine shepherd promise. Ezekiel 34 develops this extensively; John 10 declares its fulfillment in Christ. The scatter-and-gather movement of divine judgment followed by divine compassion is resolved permanently in Christ's death and resurrection: the one who "was scattered" across the nations through judgment is gathered into the one body through the Spirit (1 Corinthians 12:13; Ephesians 2:14-16). The nations hearing of the shepherd's work anticipates the Gentile mission.
11: YHWH ransoming Jacob and redeeming him from hands too strong for him — the language of padah (ransom) and gaʾal (redeem) is the vocabulary of Exodus deliverance. Paul applies both to Christ's atoning work: "you were bought with a price" (1 Corinthians 6:20, using agorazō, the Greek equivalent of padah); "our great God and Savior Jesus Christ, who gave himself for us to redeem us" (Titus 2:13-14, using lytroō, equivalent of gaʾal). The ransom and redemption from forces too strong for humanity finds its ultimate fulfillment in Christ's victory over sin and death.
12: The people coming to shout for joy on the heights of Zion, radiant over YHWH's goodness — grain, wine, oil, young animals — the eschatological feast of abundance. This is the great banquet image that Jesus uses for the kingdom (Matthew 8:11: "many will come from east and west and recline at table with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob in the kingdom of heaven"; Luke 14:15-24). The grain, wine, and oil that mark covenant blessing become in the NT the eschatological feast where Christ himself is the host and bread (John 6:35; 1 Corinthians 11:26).
13: The promise that the virgin shall rejoice in the dance, and the young men and the old together; I will turn their mourning into joy, give them gladness instead of sorrow — the reversal of mourning is the eschatological sign. Jesus explicitly fulfills this: at the wedding of Cana (John 2:1-11) he transforms the water of purification into wine of celebration; at Nain (Luke 7:13-15) he stops a funeral procession and restores the son to his mother. The pattern culminates in Revelation 21:4: "he will wipe away every tear from their eyes." The eschatological joy-replacement is a work Christ performs in anticipation and will complete at the end.
14: The priests filled with abundance, and my people satisfied with my goodness — the satiation of both priestly service and common life. The NT presents Christ as the one who fills all things (Ephesians 1:23; Jeremiah 4:10). The priest-satisfaction that Jeremiah promises finds its fulfillment in Christ's high-priestly ministry (Hebrews 7:25-27): he makes continual intercession, is himself the unblemished offering, and guarantees a covenant that provides permanent access to YHWH's goodness. The priestly office and the people's satisfaction are both fulfilled in the one priest who is also the sacrifice.
15: A direct prophecy: "A voice was heard in Ramah, lamentation and bitter weeping. Rachel is weeping for her children; she refuses to be comforted for her children, because they are no more." Matthew 2:18 cites this verse explicitly as fulfilled in Herod's massacre of the male infants of Bethlehem (Matthew 2:16-18). The typological logic is that the sorrow which accompanied Israel's exile (Ramah was the deportation staging point, 1 Samuel 10:2) is recapitulated in the grief that surrounds Christ's birth. The infant Christ escapes; the children of Bethlehem do not — they share in the pattern of innocent suffering that characterizes the pre-resurrection age and points to the one innocent sufferer who bears it fully.
16: YHWH's word to Rachel — keep your voice from weeping and your eyes from tears, for your work will be rewarded; they will return from the land of the enemy — is the divine word of hope spoken into inconsolable grief. This is the pattern of the resurrection: against all visible evidence, the divine word promises return from the land of death. Jesus speaks similar words: "Do not weep" (Luke 7:13; Jeremiah 8:52); "your brother will rise again" (John 11:23). The divine refusal to let grief be the final word is the precursor to the Easter proclamation.
17: The promise — there is hope for your future; your children shall return to their own territory — is the covenant guarantee against despair. Hebrews 6:19 calls hope "an anchor for the soul, sure and steadfast, entering into the inner place behind the curtain" — Christ as the high priest who has entered the heavenly sanctuary guarantees the future hope. The children returning to their own territory finds its NT counterpart in the promise that the meek shall inherit the earth (Matthew 5:5) and that Christ's people are heirs of God and fellow heirs with Christ (Romans 8:17).
18: Ephraim's confession — I was like an untrained calf; turn me that I may be turned, for you are YHWH my God — is the prayer of the one who recognizes their incapacity for self-correction and appeals entirely to divine action. The image of the untrained calf is the image of human nature apart from grace. Paul describes the same condition in Romans 7:18: "I can will what is right but I cannot do it." The prayer "turn me that I may be turned" is the prayer for regeneration — the divine initiative that the NT identifies with the Spirit's work (Titus 3:5; John 3:5-8).
19: Ephraim's further confession — after I turned, I was sorry; after I was instructed, I struck my thigh; I was ashamed and also humiliated, because I bore the disgrace of my youth — is the pattern of genuine contrition. The shame and humiliation of Ephraim's confession is the mirror of the shame language of the NT: Paul says believers are not ashamed of the gospel (Romans 1:16) because the shame of sin has been transferred to Christ (Hebrews 12:2: "despising the shame"; 2 Corinthians 5:21). Genuine repentance involves this reckoning with the full weight of past dishonor, which Christ's cross definitively covers.
20: YHWH's response — Is Ephraim my dear son? Is he a darling child? For as often as I speak against him, I do remember him still. Therefore my heart yearns for him; I will surely have compassion on him — is one of the most tender divine self-disclosures in the OT. The divine heart that "yearns" (hamu meʿai, literally "my innards are stirred") is the deep compassion that Jesus embodies in the Gospels: "when he saw the crowds, he had compassion on them" (Matthew 9:36; using splanchnizomai, the visceral-compassion word). The father's compassion in the prodigal son parable (Luke 15:20) directly echoes this verse.
21: The call to set up waymarks, make signposts; consider well the highway, the road by which you went — the returning exiles to mark the road home. The NT presents Christ himself as the road: "I am the way, the truth, and the life" (John 14:6). The "waymarks" and "signposts" of the prophetic tradition — all the types, shadows, and direct promises pointing toward Christ — are the signposts along the highway that leads to the Father. Hebrews 11 presents the whole of OT history as a path-marking toward the "better country" whose builder and maker is God.
22: The enigmatic sign — YHWH has created a new thing on the earth: a woman encompasses a man (neqevah tesovev gaver) — has generated many interpretations. The most defensible reading is that it announces a radical reversal of the social order: the exile feminized and weakened Israel ("woman"); the restoration will give Israel such security and vigor that the woman protects the man rather than fleeing — a reversal of the typical ancient world's vulnerability. At the deepest canonical level, the "new thing" (beritah ḥadashah) points to the new creation Christ inaugurates (2 Corinthians 5:17: "if anyone is in Christ, there is a new creation"). The unprecedented divine act that reverses normal categories is the pattern of the incarnation itself.
23: The restoration promise — they will use this speech in the land of Judah and in its cities when I restore their fortunes: "YHWH bless you, O habitation of righteousness, O holy mountain" — is the picture of the community greeting the land as righteous and holy after judgment and exile. The NT applies "righteousness" and "holiness" christologically: Christ is our righteousness (1 Corinthians 1:30; 2 Corinthians 5:21) and the one who sanctifies (Hebrews 2:11; Jeremiah 13:12). The restored community that speaks of righteousness and holiness as the character of its land finds its ultimate fulfillment in the new Jerusalem described in Revelation 21:27 — "nothing unclean will ever enter it."
24: The promise that farmers and those who wander with their flocks will live together in Judah — the reunification of agricultural and pastoral lifestyles in the land — represents the end of the exile's fragmentation. The NT vision of unity in Christ cuts across social and economic divisions: Galatians 3:28 ("neither Jew nor Greek, slave nor free, male and female"); Colossians 3:11. The eschatological unity of the restored community is a pattern that anticipates the unity Christ creates in his body, where formerly divided communities share one life.
25: YHWH's declaration — I will satisfy the weary soul, and every languishing soul I will replenish — is a direct anticipation of Jesus's invitation: "Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest" (Matthew 11:28). The weary and languishing soul that Jeremiah presents as the object of divine satiation is precisely the condition of those to whom Jesus directs his call. The satisfaction YHWH promises is the rest Christ provides — not merely comfort but genuine renewal of the exhausted soul by the one who is himself rest (Hebrews 4:9-10).
26: The mysterious parenthetical — at this I awoke and looked, and my sleep was pleasant to me — marks the dream-reception of this prophecy. The sleep and waking frame suggests the vision of restoration was given in a dream-like state, a mode of revelation prominent in the OT (Genesis 28:10-17; Daniel 7:1). The NT fulfillment of this prophetic vision is not a vision but a reality: what Jeremiah received as a dream-vision, the apostles witnessed in the flesh. John 1:14 ("we beheld his glory") is the waking counterpart to Jeremiah's pleasant dream.
27: The promise — I will sow the house of Israel and the house of Judah with the seed of men and the seed of beasts — is the replanting of the people and the land after the devastation. The seed/sowing imagery runs through the NT as a Christological motif: the Parable of the Sower (Matthew 13:3-23) describes the divine word as seed; John 12:24 uses the dying grain as the image of Christ's death and resurrection. The replanting of Israel that Jeremiah announces is the agricultural form of the resurrection hope — out of the dead soil of exile, new life springs through divine initiative.
28: The reversal promise — as I have watched over them to pluck up and to break down, to overthrow and to destroy and to bring harm, so I will watch over them to build and to plant — is the direct reversal of the prophet's own commission in Jeremiah 1:10 ("to pluck up and to pull down, to destroy and to overthrow, to build and to plant"). The same divine intentionality that brought judgment will now bring restoration. Paul applies this reversal to the cross: "he who was delivered up for our transgressions was raised for our justification" (Romans 4:25). The same God who brought the death of judgment is the same who brings the life of resurrection.
29: The abolition of the sour grapes proverb — "the fathers have eaten sour grapes and the children's teeth are set on edge" — announces a new era of direct individual accountability (paralleled in Ezek 18). The NT develops the tension between corporate solidarity and individual accountability through the lens of Christ: in Adam, all die (corporate solidarity with humanity's fall); in Christ, each one who believes is individually justified (Romans 5:12-21). The proverb's abolition points to the day when each individual stands directly before YHWH — which the NT identifies as the final judgment before Christ (2 Corinthians 5:10).
30: The positive statement — everyone who eats the sour grapes, his own teeth will be set on edge; everyone shall die for his own iniquity — establishes individual moral responsibility as the governing principle. This is the justice principle that the cross both upholds and transcends: Christ's atonement is not an evasion of individual accountability but its satisfaction — the penalty each person deserved was borne by the one who bore it representatively (2 Corinthians 5:21; 1 Peter 3:18). Individual responsibility is taken with full seriousness, and precisely because it is, the cross is costly.
32: The new covenant's second element — not like the covenant I made with their fathers when I took them out of Egypt, which they broke though I was a husband to them — establishes the contrast with the Sinai covenant. Hebrews 8:9 quotes this verse as the ground for arguing that the Levitical priesthood was provisional: the old covenant that was broken required a new covenant. The point is not that YHWH failed but that the external-law covenant was by design provisional — a tutor (Galatians 3:24-25) that pointed forward to the internal-law covenant inaugurated by Christ's Spirit-giving ministry.
33: The new covenant's third element — I will put my law within them and write it on their hearts; I will be their God and they shall be my people — is the heart of the new covenant promise. Paul quotes this in 2 Corinthians 3:3: "you are a letter from Christ... written not with ink but with the Spirit of the living God, not on tablets of stone but on tablets of human hearts." The internalization of the law is the Spirit's work: Romans 8:4 says the righteous requirement of the law is fulfilled in those who walk by the Spirit. The new covenant is inaugurated by Christ and implemented by the Spirit he gives.
34: The new covenant's fourth element — I will forgive their iniquity and I will remember their sin no more — is the ultimate basis for the entire covenant. Hebrews 10:17-18 quotes this verse as the proof that the new covenant has made the old sacrificial system obsolete: "where there is forgiveness of these, there is no longer any offering for sin." The permanent, non-recalled forgiveness is what makes the new covenant genuinely new — not a temporary covering (as the Levitical sacrifices were) but a definitive removal. Christ's once-for-all sacrifice is the event that produces this permanent forgiveness (Hebrews 9:26; Jeremiah 10:14).
35: The cosmic guarantees — if this fixed order of the sun, moon, and stars departs from before me, then the offspring of Israel shall cease to be a nation before me — grounds covenant faithfulness in the stability of creation. Paul applies this logic to the irrevocability of God's gifts in Romans 11:29: "the gifts and calling of God are irrevocable." The cosmic-stability argument establishes that the new covenant is as permanent as creation itself — and ultimately, Christ is the one through whom all things hold together (Colossians 1:17), so the guarantor of the covenant is the one who sustains the cosmos.
36: The extension of the cosmic guarantee — if the heavens above can be measured and the foundations of the earth below can be explored, I will cast off all the offspring of Israel for all they have done — is YHWH's emphatic no to Israel's permanent rejection. Paul's "Has God rejected his people? By no means!" (Romans 11:1) is the direct NT answer to the question this verse implicitly raises. The immeasurability of the heavens guarantees the indefeasibility of the covenant — which the NT locates in Christ as the one in whom all the promises are Yes (2 Corinthians 1:20).
37: The promise to rebuild Jerusalem from the Tower of Hananel to the Corner Gate — the specific geographical markers of the city's restoration — points to the eschatological city. Revelation 21:10-21 describes the New Jerusalem descending from heaven, with specific dimensions and gates — the spiritual fulfillment of Jeremiah's geographical promise. The rebuilt city of Jeremiah 31 is a historical type of the eternal city built by God, whose builder and maker is God (Hebrews 11:10).
38: The measuring line that goes straight to the hill Gareb and then shall turn to Goah — specific restoration geography for areas that were garbage dumps and waste places outside the walls — is the expansion of the holy city into previously defiled areas. The NT redemption of "unclean" areas is the theme of Acts 10-11 (the Gentile mission breaking purity barriers) and Revelation 21:27 (the New Jerusalem where nothing impure can enter precisely because impurity has been removed). The desolate places become holy places in the eschatological city.
39: The valley of dead bodies and ashes — the whole valley of the dead bodies and ashes, and all the fields as far as the brook Kidron — being made holy to YHWH represents the transformation of the place of death and defilement into sacred space. The Kidron Valley is where Jesus crossed on his way to Gethsemane (John 18:1) — the last boundary before his arrest and death. The valley associated with death and defilement becomes the path of the Son of God going to his atoning death, transforming the place of death into the ground of life.
40: The final promise — it shall not be plucked up or overthrown anymore forever — is the seal of permanence on the new Jerusalem. Revelation 21:4: "death shall be no more, neither shall there be mourning, nor crying, nor pain anymore, for the former things have passed away." The city that shall never again be plucked up or overthrown is the eternal city of God, whose permanence is secured not by political strength but by the permanent atonement of Christ and the permanent presence of God. The "forever" of the new Jerusalem finds its guarantee in the "forever" of Christ's priesthood (Hebrews 7:24) and resurrection (Romans 6:9).
